generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same state of estrangement that has kept us apart for these five long, bitter years. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the quiet resolve of one who knows that the wind may take his breath, and the sea may claim him, and yet still feels the need to say something—anything—before it is too late.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. Not the kind that merely shakes the windows or rattles the shutters. This is a storm that has been building in the air for days, a low growl in the sky that has grown into a roar. The sea is restless, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the lighthouse, though built to stand against the elements, feels as fragile as the old man who tends it. I do not know if I will see the dawn, and if I do, I do not know if I will have the strength to write this letter, or if it will be found in the wreckage of the lighthouse, or if it will be lost to the sea.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the beacon, but the light of my heart. I remember the day you left, the way the sea seemed to hold its breath, as though it, too, feared what was to come. You were so young then,

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart both heavy and resolute, for the night wind howls with a fury that even the sturdiest of men might find cause to tremble. The sea, ever restless, has stirred with a tempest that promises to test the very bones of this old lighthouse. I have spent my life watching the horizon, reading the moods of the sky, and yet I find myself unable to read the moods of my own soul. It has been five years since we last spoke, and I fear that even if I were to call upon you now, the echo of my voice would be lost in the storm.

I have always believed that the sea has a voice, and that it speaks to those who listen. I have listened, as you once did, and I have been taught to trust in the silence between the waves. But the silence between us has grown long, and I fear that even the sea cannot bridge the chasm that has formed between us.

You were a child of the light, Eleanor, and I was a guardian of it. We were meant to walk the same path, yet you chose to turn your face from the beacon, and I, in my pride, allowed you to go. I have spent these years in the company of the wind and the tide, and in their company, I have found a measure of peace. Yet I cannot deny that in the stillness of the night, when

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast at my door, the sea roiling in a fury that would make even the most seasoned sailor weep. The lantern burns low, its light flickering like a dying ember, and I find myself staring at the storm as if it might answer the questions that have haunted me for five long years.

You were once my greatest pride, my light in the darkness. But the world has changed, and so have I. We have grown apart like the tides—sometimes calm, sometimes raging. I’ve tried, Eleanor. I’ve tried to reach out, to mend what was broken, but the silence between us has been louder than any words. I’ve carried the weight of that silence, and it has worn me down like the salt upon my skin.

Tonight, I fear the sea may take more than it has already. The storm is unlike any I’ve seen in my years here, and I’ve spent my life learning to read the sea’s temper. But tonight, it speaks in a language I cannot understand. I have no one to call in the event of my demise, no one to remember me by. The thought haunts me, and I find myself wondering if I should have fought harder to keep the connection we once had.

I know you’ve had your own battles, your own storms. I’ve heard the rumors, the whispers of what you’ve endured. I do not blame

My Dearest Eliza,  

The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea roars as if it seeks to devour the very bones of the earth. I sit here by the lamp, its flame flickering like a dying ember, and I think of you. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the ache of your absence lingers as though it were but yesterday. I have not spoken to you, not in words, not in letters—only in the silence that grows heavier with each passing day.  

The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse. I have stood guard through many tempests, but this one feels different—more cruel, more desperate. The waves crash with a fury that seems to echo the storm in my soul. I have not been able to sleep, not since the day you left. I have not spoken of it, not even to myself, for fear that the words might break me.  

I have always believed that the sea is a mother, and that the lighthouse is her child, guiding the lost back to shore. But now, I wonder if the sea is weeping, and if I am but a fool to think I can stand against her wrath. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I have seen men and women brave as the storm, but I fear that this time, I may not be strong enough.  

You were always so curious

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

My dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you in the same spirit as I am in. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I have often wondered if you still remember me at all. The world moves on, and so do we, though I fear I have moved on more than I should.

You were but a girl when I last saw you, and now I wonder if you have become a woman I no longer recognize. I have not written to you, nor have I called you, and I know I have no right to expect you to forgive me. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast and the sea roars with fury, I find myself unable to sleep, and the words come to me like a prayer.

The storm is coming, Eliza. I have been keeping the light for over twenty years now, and I have seen many storms. Some pass without a whisper, and others come with the fury of the gods. This one, I fear, is of the latter. The waves are already climbing the cliffs, and the wind has begun to shake the very stones of this old tower. I have lit the lamp, and I have made my peace with the fact that I may not make it through the night.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be brave, but I have also always believed that a father must

*The wind howls outside the lighthouse, rattling the windows like the devil is trying to get in. I light another candle, its flickering flame casting long shadows across the walls. The sea is restless, churning with fury. I've been here for twenty years, through every storm, every gale, every night that the world forgot to turn. But tonight... tonight feels different. I can feel it in my bones.*

**My Dearest Clara,**

It's been five years since I've put pen to paper, and five years since I've seen your face. I know you've been writing to me too—those letters never arrived. I always thought it was the post, but now I'm not so sure. Maybe it's me. Maybe I've been too stubborn, too proud, too afraid to reach out. You were right, I suppose. I did turn my back on you.

I've been keeping this lighthouse for years, but I've never felt so alone as I do now. The storm's coming, and I'm not sure I'll make it through the night. I've seen the sea do terrible things, but this... this feels like something else. Like it's not just the wind or the waves. It's like the sea is angry, and it's angry at me.

I don't know if you'll read this, but I have to say it. I've spent so much time trying to protect the light, to

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write with a hand that trembles—not from age, but from the weight of years unspoken and the wind that howls like a beast through the cliffs. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is dark with a storm that promises to be one of the worst this season. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but I cannot shake the feeling that this time, it may not be enough.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this place, and since you left me. I have not heard from you, not a single letter, not a word. I have tried to write, but the words have always seemed to fall short of what I wanted to say. You were my light, once. Now, I fear I have become a shadow, and you have moved on, as you always did.

I know you have your own life, your own family, your own world. I understand that. But I cannot help but wonder if you think of me at all. Do you remember the nights when we sat by the fire and you would read to me? Do you remember the way you used to laugh, even when the wind howled outside? I miss that sound, more than the sea.

The storm is coming, and I am not sure I will survive it. I have no one to turn

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not presume. Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have spent them in silence, save for the wind and the sea. The lighthouse has been my refuge, my companion, and my burden. It is a place where the world seems to hold its breath, and yet, tonight, it feels as though the very heavens are about to tear asunder.

I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the knowledge that I may not live to see the dawn. A storm is gathering on the horizon, dark as the void between stars, and the sea, which has always been my friend, now roars like a beast in pain. The waves are rising, higher and faster than I have ever seen, and the wind howls with a voice that sounds like the wails of the damned. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, but I fear it may not be enough.

You remember the night I found you standing at the door, your eyes red and your hands trembling, not with fear, but with something far worse—anger, perhaps, or sorrow. I remember the way you looked at me, not as a father, but as a stranger. You said you could not stay, and I said nothing. I have never spoken to you since.

But tonight, as I stand alone in this tower

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to thee with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long winters since we last exchanged words, and the silence between us has grown as vast as the sea that surrounds this lonely lighthouse. I pray that thou art well, and that the world beyond these cliffs has not turned its back on thee.

The night is dark, and the wind howls like a banshee, a sound that stirs memories of our last parting. I recall the day thou didst leave, with eyes full of sorrow and a heart burdened by secrets. I could not bear to ask thee why, for I feared the truth would break me. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow wilder, I find myself wondering if thou art somewhere safe, or if thou art also battling tempests of thy own.

I have kept this lighthouse with unwavering devotion, as I have done for many a year. The light burns bright, and the sea obeys its call. But I have grown old, and the burden of this duty weighs heavier upon me. I have no son to take up the mantle, and with each passing day, I fear I may not see the dawn of another storm.

I have heard tell that thou art married now, and that thou hast a child. I would have been a father to that child, but I have been too far away, too lost in

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. It has been five long, silent years since our last conversation, and I have often wondered if you even remember me. I have tried to write to you, but the sea has been unkind, and the winds have carried away my words before they could reach you. I suppose I should not be surprised. After all, we have not spoken in five years, and I have not seen you in over a decade. Perhaps you have moved on, found a life beyond the shores of this lonely island where I have made my home. Perhaps you have forgotten me, or perhaps you never wanted to remember.

I write to you now not with the hope of a reply, but with the need to say something—anything—before the storm comes. I have spent many nights staring at the horizon, watching the waves crash against the rocks, wondering if I would ever see the light of day again. Tonight, however, the sea is restless, and the sky is dark with clouds that have not been seen in years. The wind howls like a beast, and I fear it may be the last night I will ever see.

You know, Eliza, that I have always been a man of duty. I have tended this lighthouse for over twenty years, and I have never once faltered in my duty. But I have also always known that I was not the man you once

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*To My Dearest Elara,*

If you're reading this, then I've either gone mad, or the sea has taken me. Either way, I hope you're safe. I hope you've found peace, or at least, some kind of peace. I don't know what's become of you, but I've spent these past five years thinking about you every damn day.

I used to think I could be a better father. I used to think that if I just worked harder, if I just gave more, if I just tried harder, you'd have come back. But you didn't. You left. And I let you. I didn't chase you, didn't beg you to come back, didn't even write. I just... sat here, watching the waves, hoping you'd come back, hoping I'd be there when you did.

But I wasn't. I let the years pass, and I let you go.

And now, the storm is coming. I've seen the sky turn black, the wind howl like a beast, the sea rise like it's trying to swallow the world. I've been here for thirty years, and I've seen worse. But this... this feels different. Like the sea is angry, and it's not just at me. It's at the world. At everything.

I don't know if I'll make it through this. I don't know if I'll

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

If you are reading this, then I have not made it through the night. Or perhaps I have, and the sea has claimed me in its merciless embrace. Either way, I write this with the ink of my own tears, and the weight of the storm pressing down on my chest like the very sky above.

Five years have passed since you left me, Eliza. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of the lighthouse standing alone in the dark, its beacon burning through the fog like a ghost of what we once were.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me. I do not know if you will ever understand why I chose to stay here, alone, with the sea and the wind and the cold. But I must say this, for if I do not, I will die with the burden of my silence.

You were my light, Eliza. You were the reason I kept the lighthouse lit, even when the world outside seemed to have forgotten the stars. You were the reason I held on to hope, even when the sea took everything else from me.

I know you think I chose the lighthouse over you. That I chose duty over love. But I did not. I chose to protect you. I chose to keep you safe. I chose to believe that the world was too cruel, too broken, for you to bear the weight of it.

You were only twelve when you left. You said

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart full of memories that have long been buried beneath the weight of silence. It has been five years since we last spoke, and I find myself wondering if I should write at all—or if the words will only bring more pain. But tonight, as I stand upon the cliff, the wind howling like a wounded beast and the sea roaring with a fury I have never known, I feel compelled to reach out to you, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that my voice may still be heard.

The storm is upon us, Eleanor. It is the kind of storm that does not merely shake the earth—it shakes the soul. The sky is black as ink, the wind is a living thing, and the sea, that ever-humble companion of the shore, now roars with a voice that could shatter the very rocks. I have been here for many years, tending the light, watching the waves, and now I fear that this night may be my last. The lighthouse is old, and the lantern is weak, and the sea, in its wrath, may take more than it gives.

You were always the daughter who asked questions, Eleanor. You asked about the stars, about the sea, about the meaning of things. You had a mind that could see beyond the veil of the present. I used to think that was a gift. Now

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with the weight of years, and with the tempest raging without, as though the very heavens themselves are in turmoil, mirroring the storm within my soul. It has been five long years since our last exchange, and I have often wondered if the silence between us was but a reflection of the silence that once lay between our hearts.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, tending to the flame that guides the lost and the weary upon the sea. It is a duty I have taken upon myself with solemn pride, for the light of the lighthouse is not merely a beacon for ships, but a symbol of hope, of steadfastness, of the enduring will to endure.

Tonight, the winds grow fierce, the skies darken with a fury that has not been seen in these parts for many a moon. The waves rise like the wrath of some unseen god, and I fear that the night may not be kind to us. I have watched the sea rage with the knowledge that I may not see dawn. And in that knowledge, I find myself thinking of you—of the daughter I once held close, and who now seems so far away.

I have tried, in my own way, to be a father to you, though I know the fault lies not with me, but with the distance we have allowed to grow. I have thought of you often, and with each passing year, the memory of

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the lighthouse, the letter is written in a steady hand, though the ink trembles slightly. The paper is yellowed with age, and the words are penned in a voice that is both weary and resolute.]**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling at my back and the sea roaring like a beast in pain. The sky is bruised with clouds, and the air is thick with the scent of salt and storm. It is not the first time I have faced such a night, but tonight feels different—more final, more like the end of something I cannot quite name.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of unanswered questions, of a house that feels like a tomb and a lighthouse that stands as a sentinel over the void. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even in my dreams. Yet here I am, writing to you now, as if the weight of the world might somehow be eased by the sound of your voice.

I have always been a man of duty, of routine, of the light that guides the lost. But you were never lost to me. You were the light I once believed I could not bear to lose. And I, a man who has stood alone on this cliff for years, have let you go. I have let you go, Eleanor, and I have not spoken of it

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and the wind howls like a beast outside the lighthouse, as though it too knows the weight of the silence between us. Five years have passed since you left, and though I've tried to carry on, the sea has whispered your name more than once, and I have heard it.

The storm is coming, and it is not the first time I have stood here, watching the sky darken with fury, knowing that the sea may take me. But tonight, it feels different. Tonight, I feel the weight of your absence more than ever.

I have often thought of you, Eleanor, in the quiet hours when the world is still and the waves are calm. I have seen your face in the moonlight, in the mist, in the reflection of the sea. You were my light, my guiding star, and I have lost it.

I know you think I have forgotten you, that I have moved on, that I have found peace in this lonely life. But I have not. Every day, I think of you. I remember the way you laughed, the way you danced, the way you kissed me in the garden when we were children. I remember the promise we made, that we would always be together, that no storm could ever separate us.

But we were separated, Eleanor. And I have not spoken to you in five years. I have not written, nor have

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches more than I can bear. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the cliffs, I find myself wondering if this will be the last time I ever hold a pen.

I’ve spent these years in this lighthouse, watching the sea with a gaze that has grown cold and hard with time. I’ve kept the light burning through every tempest, through every howling gale, through every night that the world seemed to forget us. But tonight... tonight, I fear the sea may forget us.

You were right to leave, Eliza. I was not the man you needed. I was not the father you deserved. I let the work of this place consume me, and in doing so, I lost sight of what truly mattered—my family, my daughter. I have no excuse for the silence, for the bitterness that grew between us. I have no words to undo the pain I caused, but I do have this: I want to make it right, if I still have the chance.

The storm is coming. The wind is already howling like a beast, and the sea is rising, black and furious. I’ve seen the waves before, but never like this. I’ve lit the lamp, and I’ve made sure the light is visible from the

*The wind howls through the cracks in the lighthouse walls, rattling the old windows like a thousand ghosts trying to get in. I sit by the fire, the glow of the lamp casting long shadows across the walls. The sea roars outside, and I can feel the weight of the storm pressing against the stone. I reach for the letter I’ve been writing for weeks, the ink smudged from my trembling hands. The words come slowly, each one a battle against the silence that has settled between us.*  

**My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it. I’ve been meaning to write for weeks, but the sea has been too loud, and my hands have been too shaky. You remember how you used to say that the lighthouse was like a lullaby, that it kept you calm when the world was too much. I used to think you were right.  

But I haven’t heard from you in five years, and I haven’t spoken to you in five years. I’ve been trying to forget, trying to pretend that the silence wasn’t a choice, that it wasn’t a wall I built between us. But tonight… tonight I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night. The storm is worse than anything I’ve seen in my years here. The waves are like fists against the rocks, and the wind is howling like it wants to tear the lighthouse down.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay you'll be more inclined to read it when you've had a proper meal and a decent night's rest. It is now past midnight, and the wind howls like a wounded beast upon the cliffs. The sea, that restless and unyielding thing, rages against the rocks with a fury that makes me feel as though the very earth is trembling beneath me. I write this with a trembling hand, for I fear the night may not end as it began.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I confess I have often wondered whether you still remember me at all. I have not been the father you once knew, and I fear I may never be again. But tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. It is strange, this feeling that the world is falling apart, and yet the only thing that matters is whether you are safe.

I have always told you that the light must shine, no matter the storm. It is our duty, as keepers of the light, to guide those who sail the perilous waters. But tonight, I wonder if I shall be the one to go out. The waves are rising, the wind is tearing at the lighthouse with a strength that has not been seen in decades. I have no doubt that if the tower falls, it will be the end of

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it does. You’ve been gone five long years, and I’ve been trying to forget you, but the sea has a way of reminding me of things I’d rather not remember. I write this not because I expect an answer, but because I must. Because if I don’t, I fear I’ll be left with nothing but the silence of the lighthouse and the howl of the wind.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I’ve seen the sky darken like a man with a grudge. The waves are rising, and the wind is howling like a beast that has been denied its prey. I’ve spent my life watching the sea, and I know its temper. But this—this is something else. I’ve never seen the sea behave this way. I’m not sure I’ll survive it, Eliza. And if I do, I don’t know what I’ll be left with.

You remember when I first took you to the lighthouse? You were barely six, and I thought I’d found a way to protect you from the world. But the world has a way of slipping through even the strongest hands. You left, and I didn’t stop you. I didn’t want to. I thought you needed to find your own path, and I thought I’d be here, waiting. But you didn’t come back. And I didn’t write. I

My Dearest Eliza,

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I fear the winds may have carried more than mere whispers to your door. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I confess, I have often thought of you. The sea, as ever, is a fickle mistress, and the nights here in this lighthouse are long and lonely. I have been keeping the light burning, as I have done for many a year, and I pray that the world still remembers our little corner of the world.

I have heard that you have taken up with a certain Mr. Thomas Hargrove. I hope he treats you well. I do not pretend to understand the ways of the young, but I know that love is a fickle thing, like the sea. You were always a headstrong girl, and I dares not say I was not the best of fathers. But I do hope you are happy, for that is all I have ever wanted for you.

Tonight, however, brings a storm unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sky is black as a crow's wing, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that seems to mock the very heavens. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have made preparations to weather the night. But I fear that even the light may not be enough. I have no one to turn to but the sea, and I have no wish to see

**Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the midst of your own storm, whether of heart or soul. I have not written to you in five years, and I do not know if you will even read these words. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast and the sea roars with fury, I feel the need to speak, to be heard, even if only by the waves.

You were a child when you left, so young, so full of fire and dreams. I remember the day you said goodbye. You stood at the edge of the cliff, your eyes bright with the promise of the world ahead, and I could not bear to look at you. You were everything I had ever wanted for you—bold, clever, and unafraid. But I was afraid for you, Eliza. Afraid that the world would not be kind to you, that it would break you as it did so many others.

I have spent these years alone in this lighthouse, watching the sea and the sky, listening to the whispers of the wind. It has been a lonely life, but it has also been a quiet one, one that has taught me the value of patience and the strength of silence. I have grown old, Eliza, and with every passing year, I have come to understand that the greatest storms are not always those that rage outside, but those that brew within

**To My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with regret. Five years have passed since I last saw you, and the silence between us has grown like the storm that now gathers on the horizon. I’ve spent these years watching the sea, listening to its whispers, and hoping that one day, you might return. But now, as the wind howls and the sky darkens, I fear that time may not be on my side.

I’ve always been a man of the sea, Elara. I’ve stood here on these cliffs, weathering tempests and tides, and I’ve learned to endure. But tonight is different. The storm is not just a storm—it is a reckoning. The waves are rising, the wind is screaming, and I can feel the lighthouse itself trembling beneath me. I don’t know if I’ll live through this, and if I do, I don’t know if I’ll have the strength to write another word.

I’ve thought of you often, my child. I’ve dreamed of you, of the days when you were little and would run up to me with your hands full of sand and your eyes full of wonder. I remember the way you used to laugh, the way you used to call me “Papa,” and how I would promise you that I would always be there. But I let you go, Elara. I let you go when I should have held

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that brews beyond the cliffs. The sea roars like a beast awakened, and the wind howls as though it seeks to tear the very sky asunder. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again. But tonight, as the heavens darken and the waves grow wilder, I must try to say what has long been unsaid.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light that guided me through the darkest nights of my life. I remember the day you left—how the sea was calm, the sky was clear, and yet I felt the storm of your absence in my bones. I did not understand then why you chose to walk away, and I still do not. But I have spent these years watching the beacon burn, and I have spent them wondering if I had failed you in some way.

I have not been a good father. I have been a man of habit, of duty, of silence. The sea has always been my companion, and I thought it would be easier to live with the silence than to face the noise of my own heart. But now, as the storm comes, I feel it again—this ache, this longing, this regret.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this letter with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned as I stand upon the cliff, the light of the lighthouse flickering like a dying soul. It is the night of the storm, and the sea roars like a beast awakened from its slumber. I have watched the waves rise higher and higher, crashing against the rocks with a fury that seems to mock the very heavens. I have seen the sky darken to a shade of ink, and the stars are swallowed by the clouds, leaving only the pale moon to shine upon this terrible night. I fear for my life, Eleanor, and I fear for the life of this lighthouse, and I fear for the lives of those who might be caught in the storm’s path.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written. I do not know if you will read these words, or if they will reach you at all. But I must write, for I have something to say, and I have something to confess.

You were once my greatest joy, my brightest light. You were the daughter I held close to my heart, and I would have given the world to see you smile, to hear your laughter, to feel your presence in this world. But something changed. You

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write to you now, not with the hope of finding you, but with the solemn duty of a man who has long since ceased to be a father. **My Dearest Elara,**

Five years. That's how long it's been since I last saw you. Five years since I walked you to the train station, my hands gripping the straps of your suitcase like I might hold you back from the life you chose. I never said goodbye, not really. I just stood there, watching the train disappear into the distance, and hoped you'd find your way to happiness. I hoped you'd find your way back to me.

But you didn't. And I didn't call. I didn't write. I didn't even dare to think about you. I told myself you were better off without me. That you'd be happier, that you'd have a life, that I was just a man who had nothing left to offer.

**April 3rd, 1892**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not presume that you still care to hear from me. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have yet to decide whether it was the fault of the sea or the silence between us. I suppose it was both.

I write to you now not out of some sudden longing, but because the storm is coming. It is dark, and the wind howls like the devil himself, and I fear I may not see the dawn. I have kept the light burning all night, as I always do, though it seems a cruel jest to keep the light on when I may not be here to tend it. I have been watching the waves for days, and they grow more furious each hour. I have no doubt the sea will take me, as it has taken so many before me.

You were always so quick to judge me, Eleanor. You said I was a fool to stay here, that I had no future, no life beyond this lonely rock. You said I had no soul. But I have a soul, and I have a life, and I have you. I have always had you. And yet, I have not been able to reach you, not in all these years.

I remember the day you left. You were so angry, so hurt, and I could not bear to look at you. You said

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this missive finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear the winds may have carried your letters away, as they have mine. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the days when we were a family. The sea has been a cruel mistress, and I find myself more alone than ever.

Tonight, as I write these words, the sky is dark and heavy with storm clouds, and the waves roar like the voices of the dead. I have seen the light from my lantern many times, but never have I felt it so dim, so distant, as I do now. I fear that the tempest may take me before I can see another dawn. And if I do not return, I beg you, my dear, to remember that I loved you with all my heart, and that I have always been your father.

I have no more to say, save that I am sorry for the years we have lost, and I pray that the sea will carry my words to you, and that you will forgive me for not being the man I once was.

With deepest love and sorrow,  
Your Father,  
A. M. B.  

P.S. If you should read this and I am not with you, know that I was a good man, and that you are the light of my life.
